notes: Marched through tourist-heavy areas chanting "tourists go home," fired water pistols at visitors. Led by the Assemblea de Barris pel Decreixement Turístic, a coalition of 100+ local organizations

value_raw: Second, coordinated round hit Barcelona and Mallorca simultaneously — first coordinated cross-city effort by southern-European overtourism activists
notes: The most visible, most organized, longest-running anti-tourism/anti-foreigner-arrival movement found anywhere in this project — a genuinely sharper edge (physical confrontation, not just placards) than Lisbon's Habita/Web Summit pattern

value_raw: Hot, mostly-dry summers; mild winters rarely below 8-10°C
notes: Not independently checked against a primary meteorological source
Numbeo/aggregate: ~€804/month excluding rent. Family of four: €3,500-5,000/month all-in

value_raw: 58 hospitals citywide (12 public, 46 private); CUAP urgent-care 24/7; Hospital Clínic de Barcelona a named 24/7 multi-language trauma center
notes: Public care carries genuinely long waits for routine/non-urgent needs despite high underlying quality; private is the usual expat workaround. No minutes-to-nearest-ER figure estimated

source_ref: [GAP — no equivalent to Lisbon's CaixaBank 49-143% finding found this pass]
notes: A real open gap, especially given Barcelona's own tourism-driven housing pressure

source_ref: [GAP — no hard figure found this pass]
notes: Same gap as Valencia's
Same gap as Valencia's

source_ref: [GAP — not found this pass; Barcelona's backlash coverage rests on protest-coverage journalism only, no first-hand lived account]
notes: The same gap Lisbon's Leanne Brady account filled for Portugal — Barcelona's file has no equivalent yet

value_raw: Typical 30-40% down payment; 12-14% added to purchase price for closing costs; 3.5-4.5% interest at 60-70% LTV
notes: The closing-cost figure runs higher than Valencia's/national's general 6-10% transfer-tax alone, reflecting added legal/mortgage/currency costs specific to non-resident buyers. Mortgage detail is a secondary line per this project's cash-only research priority, recorded for completeness only
A meaningfully worse composite score than Valencia's 35.05 — a real, sourced, differentiated finding, not "both are Spain, both are equally safe"

Las Ramblas named repeatedly as one of the single most-pickpocketed streets in Europe; Barcelona ranks #1 on at least one 2026 European pickpocketing ranking. Violent crime specifically stays genuinely low
A real, unresolved three-way spread (~€5,200/m² one estimate, €5,008/m² April 2026 asking data up 7.44% YoY, a separate ~€4,429.52/m² index figure) — the same disagreement pattern as Valencia's file
Close to a 2x spread across the city
New-build meaningfully pricier than resale — worth distinguishing rather than blending into one average
No lived account of an actual foreign buyer's purchase process found
Barcelona's most expensive rental district (~€26.0/m²); a separate neighborhood-tier cut gives €850-1,200 for the trendy tier — the two source cuts don't cleanly reconcile, stated rather than forced
Gràcia: a village-like feel, artsy, quieter. Poblenou (22@ district): rebranded tech/startup/coworking hub, long beachfront
Home-purchase costs up ~40% over the same period — the concrete economic backdrop the marches are responding to

value_raw: The active, physically-confrontational backlash makes a visible group arrival a real, live risk-shape question
notes: Not evaluated this pass — flagged as genuinely live, not neutral, unlike a typical unresearched gap

value_raw: All ~10,000 licensed STR units phased out by November 2028 — no renewals once expired
notes: Announced by Mayor Jaume Collboni June 2024; Spain's Constitutional Court upheld the plan March 2025 — a real, tested, currently standing legal outcome. Does NOT restrict long-term residential renting or buying. European Commission has separately raised an EU Services Directive concern, unresolved, hasn't overturned the ruling

Real, sustained in-person infrastructure, not a recently-arrived thin layer — a genuinely deep-bench international community, the tension being that it exists inside a population actively naming the tourism economy it's adjacent to as a housing problem
Up 12.2% year-on-year — a real, accelerating trend

value_raw: Hot summers 30°C+, mild winters rarely below 8-10°C, ~300 sunny days/year
notes: Not independently checked against a primary meteorological source
Numbeo (March 2026): ~€650/month excluding rent. Couple: €2,800-3,800/month

value_raw: 25-35% cheaper on rent, 10-15% on groceries, 15% on dining
Dense urban center; Hospital Universitario y Politécnico La Fe (public), Quirónsalud/9 de Octubre/Vithas (private, incl. dedicated International Patient Care)
notes: Emergency line 112, multilingual, treatment regardless of insurance status. No specific minutes-to-nearest-ER figure found
A genuinely deep bench of existing foreign community, not a nascent scene

source_ref: [GAP — no equivalent to Lisbon's CaixaBank 49-143% finding found this pass]
notes: A real open gap, not a confirmed absence of a premium
College of Registrars data — mainly EU nationals (57.32% of foreign sales), UK citizens the single largest foreign-buyer nationality (7.96% of all purchases)

source_ref: [GAP — no hard figure found this pass]
notes: Same gap this project's Portugal research flagged for Lisbon/Porto

source_ref: [GAP — no location-specific measured figure found, national average used as a floor]
notes: Not researched this pass.

One of the deadliest natural disasters in modern Spanish/European history — ~15,000 displaced, 78 municipalities severely damaged. Cross-checked against a peer-reviewed public-health journal and NASA Earth Observatory satellite documentation

fact_label: Natural hazard — flood exposure, city center spared
notes: The catastrophic damage/deaths concentrated in satellite municipalities 8+ km south (Paiporta 40-60 deaths alone, Alfafar, Catarroja, Massanassa); city-proper neighborhoods (Ruzafa, Cabanyal, El Carmen, Patraix, Benimaclet) sit in a meaningfully different exposure band — a precise "country tier vs. location tier" distinction, not a blanket "Valencia flooded" claim
The honest default for a brand-new candidate's first research pass

A second source gives 31.5, directionally consistent. Meaningfully lower than Barcelona's 51.89-51.96 — one of the safer major Spanish cities on this measure. Walking-alone perception 84.88 (day)/66.37 (night)

An honest absence-of-evidence result, not a confirmed-calm one — the same discipline applied to Porto's early passes. Real rising rents (12.2% YoY) are the same underlying pressure that produces backlash elsewhere
A real, unresolved three-way spread across sources (Engel & Völkers ~€2,821, a "realistic closing price" ~€2,700, an asking-price figure €3,368 up 8.9% YoY) — not forced to one number
A real, more than 2x spread across the city
Every source is an aggregator or real-estate-marketing site — no lived first-hand buyer account found
Former fishermen's quarter, Art Nouveau tile-fronted houses, protected neighborhood under "careful gentrification" — a laid-back, sea-breeze expat community
~15 min from centre by metro — the "better value, still connected" answer
Valencia's "hipster epicentre" — most popular for young professionals/digital nomads. Prices rising consistently for several years, no longer cheap
A dense urban Mediterranean city is likely structurally different than the standalone-house/compound model — inference, not independently checked

value_raw: A real, ongoing, named gathering point
notes: Not just a generic "there are Facebook groups" claim

value_raw: Legal drinking age 18; ordinary EU-standard Mediterranean alcohol culture — wine/beer normal everyday presence, not restricted
notes: Not independently re-verified to a primary statute this pass

value_raw: Decriminalized for private use in a private residence (~100g commonly cited); public consumption/sale/trafficking illegal, fines €601-€30,000
notes: Cannabis Social Clubs (CSCs) are a real, legally gray-zone-tolerated institution on private-association/privacy-rights grounds. Framework essentially unchanged since 2023 — no imminent reform. Primary statute (Ley 4/2015) not pulled directly this pass

value_raw: Underlying constitutional-status question remains unresolved but has receded from the 2017 acute-crisis level
notes: Spain's constitutional order has held through repeated fragmented-parliament periods

value_raw: comunidad de bienes (undivided joint ownership) + propiedad horizontal (Ley 49/1960) — same functional pattern as Guatemala/Colombia/Mexico/Argentina/Portugal
notes: Sixth confirmed country following this cross-candidate compound/co-ownership pattern. Not independently verified to primary Civil Code article numbers — named consistently across property-law sources

fact_label: Convenio Especial (public-system buy-in) — eligibility and age-gated cost
value_raw: 12mo residency + not employed/self-employed in Spain required; €60/mo under 65, €157/mo 65+
notes: myspainvisa.com/movingtospain.com convergent. Excludes subsidized prescription-drug rates — the single biggest gap for chronic medication. Not free entitlement even after years of Non-Lucrative Visa (NLV) residence

Effective under Royal Decree 126/2026. +€916/month first dependent, +€305/month per additional dependent. Threshold rises automatically whenever Spain's minimum wage rises — a structural difference from Portugal's D8, whose threshold only moves on separate Portuguese action

fact_label: Digital Nomad Visa (DNV) accepts passive income as qualifying proof
notes: Requires a university degree OR 3+ years documented professional experience, employment with sponsoring company 3+ months pre-application, Spanish-sourced income capped at 20% of total — active remote-employment income, not a fit for a passive-income profile

value_raw: Up to 1yr if applied from abroad via consulate; up to 3yr if applied from inside Spain
notes: A genuinely different (better) shape than most guides' default description of the shorter consulate route

Fully operational across the Schengen Area as of April 10, 2026 — manual stamping gone, every crossing logged biometrically area-wide, closing the practical enforcement gap that previously made border-hopping quietly tolerated

fact_label: Employer-sponsored work visa / EU Blue Card
source_ref: [GAP — named for completeness, not independently verified to a primary source this pass]
notes: Requires a job offer from a Spanish-registered employer; EU Blue Card requires a salary threshold tied to Spain's average gross salary

value_raw: No minimum investment; gated on a business plan judged 'innovative'/'special economic interest,' favorable report required from Spain's foreign-investment promotion agency (ICEX)-Invest in Spain
notes: Governed by Article 70 of Law 14/2013, survived the 2025 Golden Visa reform. Initial residence authorization 3 years, renewable. Requires an actual operating business — not a fit for passive capital or buy-to-let, the same "hands-on, not passive" shape as Portugal's D2

fact_label: EU Family Member Card (spouse of Spanish/EU citizen)
source_ref: [GAP — not independently traced to primary statutory text this pass]
notes: Governed by the EU Free Movement framework, genuinely faster than the general reunification route above — flagged as an open item, same tier as Portugal's Article 15 gap
Removes the home-grown-currency-crisis risk this criterion's Argentina/Milei model case tracks — the coalition's fragility is a governance-friction fact, not a currency or expropriation risk

value_raw: Requires 12+ months legal Spanish residency (renewed initial permit) before sponsoring a spouse/children/dependent parents
notes: Under RD 1155/2024, spouses/registered partners/children 16+ get automatic work authorization alongside the reunification permit — a real, named improvement over the prior separate-work-permit requirement

value_raw: Officially discontinued April 3, 2025 (Organic Law 1/2025) — no new applications accepted
notes: Confirmed via Spain's own government transparency portal (one.gob.es). Applications filed before the cutoff still processed; existing holders can renew under original criteria. Foreign property purchase itself remains completely legal — only the automatic-residency-via-purchase mechanism is gone
valenciamove.com paraphrase of a WHO assessment; not independently traced to WHO's own primary ranking text this pass. Same phrasing repeated in `Spain/Barcelona/overview.md#healthcare`
Among the fastest averages in Europe; ~95% national fiber (FTTH) population coverage, ~79% even in rural areas

value_raw: Junts withdrew backing fall 2025 over deportation-powers dispute, itself pressured by rise of Catalan far-right party Aliança Catalana
notes: Analysts describe "continued democratic resilience with recurring coalition challenges," not systemic breakdown. General election must happen no later than August 22, 2027

source_ref: [GAP — not exhaustively checked against every Ibero-American nationality]
notes: Unlike Portugal's Community of Portuguese Language Countries (CPLP) carve-out, Spain's Ibero-America equivalent doesn't appear to grant an analogous in-country residence-application right on tourist entry — not exhaustively checked

fact_label: Non-Lucrative Visa (NLV) accepts passive income as qualifying proof
notes: Cannot work in Spain at all — no professional/business activity, remote-for-foreign-employer included. A genuinely passive-income profile (pensions, rental, investments) is the intended fit

fact_label: Non-Lucrative Visa (NLV) converts to permanent residency
notes: After 5 years legal residence (EU Long-Term Residence Directive threshold) — not yet independently verified against Spanish statutory text, same open-item shape as Portugal's Article 80 gap. Visa converts 90-day entry → 1-year residence card → 2-year renewable blocks

value_raw: Private health insurance valid 1yr, covering 100% of costs, zero copayments — mandatory
notes: Confirmed directly against Spain's Ministry of Foreign Affairs consular page (exteriores.gob.es) — same primary source as the Non-Lucrative Visa (NLV) income-threshold row. NLV holders cannot work in Spain, so this is their only healthcare access for at least their first year

value_raw: Floated (100% transfer tax OR outright non-EU non-resident buyer ban); NOT law, no vote, no implementation date, minority government lacks votes
notes: The single most consequential open political question found in this Spain research for a non-EU, non-resident buyer profile specifically — legal foundation disputed at both Spanish constitutional and EU level, explicitly not treated as settled either direction

Directly confirmed against Spain's Ministry of Foreign Affairs consular page (exteriores.gob.es). +100% of Spain's multi-purpose public income indicator (IPREM) per additional family member. IPREM is €600/month for 2026 (unchanged from 2025 — Spain never approved a new national budget)

Comparable in shape to Portugal's €80-€700 Article 192 schedule, though Spain runs higher at the low end. Expulsion carries a 3-10yr Schengen-wide entry ban, but CJEU guidance says a simple irregular stay with no aggravating factor should generally draw a fine, not expulsion

fact_label: Pet import (dog and cat) — rabies titer / quarantine requirement
value_raw: No divergence found between species; microchip THEN rabies vaccine 21+ days before travel; NO titer required for US-origin pets; NO quarantine with complete documentation
notes: A ninth exception to this project's dog-only pet-import gap. Genuinely easy — on par with or better than Portugal's already-easy path. Up to 5 companion animals/person per Law 7/2023

value_raw: New EU non-commercial pet-travel health certificates take effect Oct 1, 2026 (commercial certs Oct 17, 2026)
notes: EU Regulation 2026/131 — a paperwork-format change, not a new substantive barrier, worth timing a move around if near this window

value_raw: Most mainstream insurers cap 65-75 (Sanitas 75, Adeslas 70, Asisa 69); minority extend further (ASSSA 79, Cigna no limit)
notes: A commercial insurer decision, not a Spanish immigration-law age limit — Spain sets no statutory Non-Lucrative Visa (NLV) age cap. Real compound gate since private cover is the NLV's own hard issuance requirement, not optional

value_raw: No nationality-based geographic restriction — any foreigner, resident or not, can buy anywhere including coastal/border areas
notes: Same open-freehold shape as Portugal, Morocco, Belize — unlike Guatemala's 200m lakeshore band or Mexico's Restricted Zone

No nationality-based geographic restriction — any foreigner, resident or not, can buy anywhere including coastal/border areas. Live political trajectory, not modeled as an enacted mechanism: a floated 100% transfer tax or outright non-EU non-resident buyer ban is NOT law, has had no vote, no implementation date, and the minority government lacks the votes.
Same open-freehold shape as Portugal, Morocco, Belize.
No Portugal-Article-72-style formal extension found — 90 days is the hard limit, a longer stay requires an actual visa/residence route

value_raw: 90 days within any rolling 180-day period (Schengen, area-wide)
notes: Not reset by leaving and re-entering Spain specifically — calculated across the entire Schengen Area. Same shared clock as Portugal/Crete

fact_label: Transaction-cost load (transfer tax, foreign buyer, resale)
notes: Varies by autonomous region.
